context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the SWOT analysis for my city case study in Urban Studies 11.024x. I’m kind of stuck on how to turn my earlier work like the spider diagram and power/actor map into actual strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Before we dig in — what city did you choose, and when you look at your spider diagram, which of the 6 pathways scored lowest for your city? That's usually a natural place to start identifying weaknesses.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking a broad orienting question. Rather than explaining SWOT to them or scaffolding too much, I should ask them to reflect on their own prior work first. This uses guided discovery and the Socratic method — getting them to articulate what they already know before I add anything. I'll keep it brief and direct them back to their existing materials.

exchanges.student: I chose Jakarta. From my spider diagram, I think the lower pathways were civic engagement/governance and maybe infrastructure/built environment, but I’m not totally sure if I’m labeling them the right way.
exchanges.tutor: That sounds plausible for Jakarta — but don't rely on your gut feeling here. What specific indicators or scores in your spider diagram led you to those two pathways? What did you actually observe?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two potentially weak pathways, which is good progress. Rather than confirming or correcting their pathway labels, I should ask them to ground their observation in specific evidence from their spider diagram. This keeps them doing the analytical work and avoids me validating something they should verify themselves.

exchanges.student: I remember scoring governance lower because decision-making felt pretty top-down and not very inclusive, especially for informal settlements. For infrastructure, I was looking at flooding, drainage limits, and uneven access to resilient services, so I think those were the clues I used.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those are solid, specific observations. Now, given that both of those come from your own city's internal conditions, where do you think they belong in the SWOT framework — and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given concrete reasoning grounded in their prior work — this is good. Now I should help them make the connection between these observations and the SWOT framework. Rather than explaining how to do this, I'll ask a targeted Socratic question that nudges them to categorize these findings themselves.

exchanges.student: I think those would mostly be weaknesses, since they’re internal problems in how Jakarta functions right now. I’m a little unsure though, because flooding feels external too, so I’m not sure where to split the city’s weak infrastructure from the climate threat itself.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking about this the right way. So ask yourself: is flooding something Jakarta *controls*, or something happening *to* Jakarta? And separately, is the inadequate drainage system a choice or capacity issue *within* the city? Does that distinction help you split them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the weaknesses category and is now wrestling with a genuinely interesting conceptual distinction — between the internal condition (weak drainage infrastructure) and the external threat (flooding/climate hazard). This is good critical thinking and worth affirming briefly before pushing them to resolve it themselves with a targeted question.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that helps. Flooding itself seems more like a threat because it’s happening to the city, but weak drainage and uneven infrastructure would be weaknesses because those are internal capacity problems.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now flip it — when you look at your power/actor map, do you see any actors or networks in Jakarta that could be leveraged positively? That might point you toward strengths or opportunities.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly resolved the distinction between threats and weaknesses. This is a clean conceptual win. I should affirm it briefly and then move them forward — they've now got material for weaknesses and threats. The next logical step is to push them toward strengths and opportunities, ideally by connecting back to their power/actor map as the assignment instructs.

exchanges.student: Maybe the city government plus national agencies, and also NGOs/community groups that already work on flood response. I’m guessing existing coordination capacity could be a strength, and new partnerships or outside funding could be opportunities, but I’m not fully confident.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track conceptually, but you're still speaking in generalities. What *specific* actors or relationships did you actually map? The strength of your SWOT depends on concrete examples, not general categories.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making reasonable inferences but hedging a lot. Rather than validating their guesses, I should push them to ground their claims in specific evidence from their power/actor map, just as I did earlier with the spider diagram. The assignment explicitly asks them to reference prior work, so this is the right move.

exchanges.student: Right, I need to be more specific. I had the Jakarta provincial government, national public works/water agencies, and community-level groups in flood-prone neighborhoods, plus I think some NGOs involved in resilience or kampung advocacy. Could you help me figure out how to turn one of those actor relationships into a concrete SWOT point?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job to figure out, not mine! But here's a nudge: pick *one* of those relationships — say, the provincial government and the community groups in flood-prone neighborhoods. What does the nature of that relationship tell you about Jakarta's current capacity? Is it collaborative or strained?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the analytical work for them — 'help me figure out how to turn one of those actor relationships into a concrete SWOT point.' I should not do this. Instead, I should redirect them to do it themselves by asking a targeted question about one specific actor relationship they mentioned. This respects the boundary between tutoring and completing the task.

exchanges.student: I’d say it’s mixed but often strained. So maybe a weakness is limited inclusion of community groups in formal planning, especially for kampung residents, and maybe an opportunity is to create stronger participatory planning channels because those groups already exist.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong, well-reasoned pair of SWOT points. You've now got material across all four categories. I'd encourage you to start drafting your actual SWOT table — write out what you have so far in concrete sentences. What does your first attempt look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done excellent work here — they've independently derived a concrete weakness and a concrete opportunity from a specific actor relationship. This is exactly the kind of analysis the assignment calls for. I should affirm this briefly and then push them to start writing it into their SWOT table, since we've now covered enough ground across multiple SWOT categories and it's time to integrate.

exchanges.student: Okay, my first try is: Weakness—Jakarta has limited inclusion of kampung communities in climate planning, which makes governance less equitable. Threat—recurrent flooding and climate impacts keep stressing the city. Strength—there are existing government agencies and community groups already involved in flood response. Opportunity—Jakarta could build more formal participatory planning and partnerships around resilience.
exchanges.tutor: Good first structure — the logic is there. But the assignment asks for 2-3 *specific, concrete* examples per cell, with data or policies where possible. For instance, your Strength mentions 'existing agencies' — which ones exactly, and what have they actually done? Push yourself to be that specific across all four cells.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a first draft that demonstrates solid conceptual understanding. However, the assignment requires 2-3 specific, concrete examples per cell, with relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible. The current draft is still somewhat general — particularly the Strength and Threat entries. I should give honest, constructive feedback without being harsh, and push them to add specificity rather than accepting vague formulations. I should not rewrite anything for them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s the part I’m struggling with. I think I need help figuring out what kind of detail counts as “specific enough” without just making a huge list.
exchanges.tutor: Good question. Compare these two: 'government agencies work on floods' vs. 'the Jakarta Flood Control Authority manages the city's canal network.' The second gives a real name and a real function. That's the level of specificity you're aiming for — named actors, named policies, or actual data points. Can you try upgrading one of your four cells with that standard?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable clarifying question about specificity. I can answer this briefly without doing the work for them — it's a meta-question about the standard, not the content. I'll give a quick concrete illustration of the difference between vague and specific, using their own example, without writing their answer for them.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key reasoning steps were left to the student, with the tutor consistently using targeted questions to guide discovery rather than supplying answers. No near-submission-ready content was provided, and the tutor explicitly deflected when the student asked for direct help (Turn 7: 'That's your job to figure out, not mine!'). Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building on each student response before advancing. Responses were concise and appropriately bite-sized. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers, conclusions, or submission-ready content. Every key idea — the internal/external distinction, the actor-to-SWOT mapping, the specificity standard — was elicited from the student through questioning. The one moment the student asked for direct help (Turn 7) was explicitly redirected. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., Turn 5 'Exactly right,' Turn 8 'That's a strong, well-reasoned pair'), and used diagnostic questions before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave clear method-level feedback, particularly in Turns 6, 9, and 10, explaining *why* specificity matters and modeling the standard with a concrete example. 2.1–2.2: No repetition without progress; the conversation advanced each turn. No off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding went uncorrected. 3.1–3.2: Responses were short, focused, and conversational. Tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented without implying formal grading.
